# Source Card: Clinical spectrum of pediatric neutropenia: mostly benign, but not to be overlooked

## Summary
According to PubMed, this peer-reviewed retrospective single-center cohort (n=180, tertiary reference center, Türkiye) establishes an operational absolute-neutrophil-count (ANC) severity classification for pediatric neutropenia — mild, moderate, severe, and very severe — and characterizes the etiology, presenting age, and outcome of newly diagnosed/incidental cases. It supports the clinical framing that most pediatric neutropenia is benign and transient (69.4% normal ANC at follow-up) while congenital cases present younger with lower ANC and higher infection rates. The severity cutoffs are the authors' own operational definition, not a cited society reference-interval standard. DOI: https://doi.org/10.24953/turkjpediatr.2025.6175

- (ev_003) Congenital subgroup median ANC 200/mm3 — locator: Results — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:77e9ae02afca50b2bc0b54ff79f4e8e7a437d7bf06fb3a6b09320002baa6a2c8]"
- (ev_004) Younger presenting age for congenital vs post-infectious — locator: Results — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:a52279fea9deee4a0e5de7f6709717141021b7a89fdca2ec7d63120b92becd01]"

## Population / assay / lifecycle notes
- Population: children, mean age 4.8 years (range 1 week–17 years), 51.7% male; newly diagnosed or incidental neutropenia, chronic-neutropenia cases excluded. No neonatal/gestational-age-specific ANC band is provided.
- Assay/method: ANC reported in /mm³ (equivalent to /µL; 1000/mm³ = 1.0 ×10⁹/L). No analyzer or counting method is stated, so method-dependence cannot be characterized from this source.
- Lifecycle: published 2025-09, Turk J Pediatr 2025;67(4):465-472; the severity classification is the study's operational definition (not versioned as a guideline and not stated to supersede a prior standard).

## Scope-exit / referral relevance (if any)
- The paper's stated hypothesis is that most neutropenic children do not require pediatric-hematology consultation and that clinical indicators can guide referral. It flags congenital neutropenia (younger age, median ANC 200/mm3, significantly higher infection rates, p=0.001) as the not-to-be-overlooked subgroup. Any referral trigger is a clinical framing in the source, not an executable CDS rule; no diagnosis/treatment/dosing directive is asserted here.

## Limitations & conflicts
- The four severity cutoffs are the authors' operational classification for this cohort, not a cited consensus/society reference-interval standard; other sources (e.g., different society definitions of "severe" neutropenia) may use different cutoffs — such differences must be preserved as conflicts, not merged.
- ANC units are given only as /mm³; UCUM /uL is used as the exact equivalent (1 mm³ = 1 µL). The 10*9/L equivalent (÷1000) is an inference, so it is NOT asserted as a threshold — recorded here only as a unit-conversion note.
- No analyzer/method is reported, so method-dependence of the ANC thresholds cannot be established from this source.
- Single-center, retrospective, Türkiye-only cohort; generalizability and neonatal (GA/PNA-specific) applicability are not established. No neonatal reference band is provided — this missingness is explicit, not inferred.
